title: Reading and Use of English - Part 8
description: Read the four texts about the music industry. For questions 47 – 56, choose from the experts (A – D).

texts.A:
### The Producer (Alex)
Alex believes that AI will soon be the primary tool for hit-making. [52]'We're already seeing software that can predict which chord progressions will trigger a dopamine hit,' he says.[/52] [48]However, he warns that this could lead to a 'sonic homogenization' where every song on the radio sounds like it was produced by the same person.[/48] [55]He points out that the real value in the future will be 'live' experiences, which cannot be replicated by an algorithm.[/55] [51]He mentions that even now, fans are willing to pay more for a concert ticket than for a lifetime subscription to a streaming service.[/51]
texts.B:
### The Independent Artist (Jasmine)
Jasmine uses social media to bypass traditional record labels. [47]'The gatekeepers are gone, but now we're at the mercy of the algorithm,' she admits.[/47] [53]She spends more time editing short videos than actually writing songs, which she finds increasingly frustrating.[/53] [50]She mentions that her most successful track was a 'meme song' that went viral for ten seconds and then was forgotten.[/50] For Jasmine, the challenge is building a 'slow' career in a 'fast' digital world. [56]She argues that the current system favors quantity over quality.[/56]
texts.C:
### The Archivist (Dr. Aris)
Dr. Aris is concerned about the 'digital dark age' of music. [49]He points out that while we have more music than ever, the formats we use are incredibly fragile.[/49] [54]He highlights that a physical vinyl record from the 1960s is easier to play today than a digital file from the 1990s that requires obsolete software.[/54] [52]He advocates for the 'physical preservation' of sound as a cultural priority.[/52] For him, the ease of streaming has made us treat music as a disposable utility rather than a lasting piece of art.
texts.D:
### The Tech-Analyst (Kevin)
Kevin is excited about the 'democratization' of music production. [51]Now, a teenager in her bedroom can produce a track that sounds as professional as something from a multi-million dollar studio.[/51] [50]He predicts that the next big shift will be 'interactive music', where the listener can change the tempo or the instruments of a song in real-time.[/50] [48]He dismisses the fear of AI, arguing that it’s just another tool, like the electric guitar was in its day.[/48] [53]However, he admits that the sheer volume of new music being uploaded daily makes it nearly impossible for any single artist to stand out.[/53]

questions.question: mention that while old barriers have disappeared, new ones have taken their place?
questions.explanation: Jasmine says gatekeepers are gone but now she's at the mercy of the algorithm.

questions.question: express concern that technology might lead to a lack of variety in music?
questions.explanation: Alex warns of 'sonic homogenization'. (Kevin D disagrees with this fear).

questions.question: point out the irony that modern storage methods are less durable than older ones?
questions.explanation: Dr. Aris notes digital formats are fragile compared to vinyl records.

questions.question: refer to a specific type of musical content that has a very short lifespan?
questions.explanation: Jasmine mentions a 'meme song' that went viral and was then forgotten.

questions.question: note a significant difference in how consumers value different musical products?
questions.explanation: Alex mentions fans pay more for live concerts than for streaming subscriptions.

questions.question: mention a technology that can anticipate a human emotional response?
questions.explanation: Alex mentions software predicting chord progressions that trigger a 'dopamine hit'.

questions.question: complain about the amount of time spent on tasks not directly related to making music?
questions.explanation: Jasmine spends more time editing videos than writing songs.

questions.question: mention the difficulty of accessing data stored in older digital formats?
questions.explanation: Dr. Aris mentions digital files from the 90s requiring obsolete software.

questions.question: claim that certain aspects of music will remain uniquely human?
questions.explanation: Alex says 'live' experiences cannot be replicated by an algorithm.

questions.question: suggest that the current market rewards the speed of production over its value?
questions.explanation: Jasmine argues the current system favors quantity over quality.
